summary: Gruber analyzes Apple's new enforcement of in-app purchasing rules for e-book apps, triggered by Sony's iOS app rejection. Apple claims the rules haven't changed, but Gruber calls this out as double-speak since Amazon's Kindle and Barnes & Noble's Nook apps have long operated without in-app purchases. He argues Sony is being hurt for being late to the game, making them an easy first target before Apple pressures Amazon and B&N to comply. Gruber raises key unanswered questions about pricing and revenue splits, noting that a 30% cut on books would be difficult for Amazon to accept. He predicts Amazon will ultimately adopt Apple's in-app purchasing APIs rather than abandon iOS.
key_points: Apple claims rules haven't changed while simultaneously enforcing previously unenforced policies — a contradiction Gruber highlights as Orwellian double-speak
Sony's rejection is likely a strategic first step: easier to block a new app than pull established ones like Kindle and Nook
The 30% revenue cut creates a serious pricing dilemma — Amazon must either mark up prices 43% or accept 30% less revenue on in-app purchases
Apple's move is a win-win from their perspective: more revenue while arguably improving user experience with in-app purchasing
The timing coincides with Apple's upcoming announcement of subscription purchasing APIs with News Corp's The Daily
money_quotes: Translation: We haven't changed the rules, but what used to be allowed is no longer allowed.

It's easier to reject Sony's app as a first step toward the application of new rules because Sony's app is brand-new — Apple isn't taking anything away from users that was previously available to them.
You can't say it's surprising that the rules are evolving toward more money for Apple while improving the experience for users — that's win-win from Apple's perspective.
Here's the de facto rule, in a nut: If you have an app in the App Store, Apple gets a cut of the dough from the app.
It's hard to imagine Amazon accepting a 70/30 split from Apple. But I can't see Amazon pulling their iOS Kindle apps, either.
Apple's official stance seems to be that the rules haven't changed, but they weren't enforcing them until now.
key_insight: Apple's claim that 'nothing has changed' while enforcing previously ignored rules against e-book apps is strategic double-speak designed to expand its revenue cut across all App Store content purchases.
